content.headline: Cooking is already chaotic — doing your prep after you start is how things burn, get forgotten, and come out wrong.
points: Read the entire recipe before touching anything — surprises mid-cook are the enemy
Every ingredient prepped, measured, and in its own small container before you light the stove
Prep in the order that makes time sense: longest tasks first, quick ones last
When your counter shows everything the recipe needs, already done, you're ready to start
content.tell_me_more: Professional kitchens run mise en place as a non-negotiable daily ritual because speed and consistency are impossible without it. The real payoff for home cooks isn't speed — it's bandwidth. When the knife work and measuring are done, you can actually pay attention to the food: its color, its smell, what it sounds like in the pan. That attention is what makes food taste good, not the recipe.

missions.title: Full Mise en Place Spread
missions.description: Choose any recipe you've made before and do a complete mise en place — every ingredient prepped, measured, and in its own container — before turning on any heat.
missions.why_it_matters: Seeing all your prep laid out before you start trains your brain to cook proactively instead of reactively.
missions.prompt: Show your counter with every ingredient for your chosen recipe prepped and ready — diced, measured, and in separate containers. Nothing should still be whole or in its original packaging. The photo should show the full setup before any cooking begins.
reflection_choices: Everything ready, counter organized, felt genuinely calm when I started cooking
Most things prepped but still had to do a few things mid-cook
Did the prep but piled things together instead of keeping them separated
Started cooking before the prep was fully done

missions.title: Before vs. After
missions.description: Reflect on how your cooking experience felt when everything was prepped in advance versus how you normally cook.
missions.why_it_matters: Articulating what changed helps you recognize what you're actually practicing and why it's worth the extra setup time.
missions.prompt: When you were actually cooking (not prepping), what did you notice about your attention and pace compared to how you usually cook? Was there a moment where you would have normally scrambled but didn't? Describe it specifically.
